mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-Benz ML250 / ML350 diesel engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned Mercedes-Benz ML250/ML350 diesel engine costs £3,500–£6,500 supply only, varying by generation: OM651 £3,000–£5,500, OM642 (W164) £3,500–£6,000, OM642 (W166) £4,000–£7,000. Fitting adds £1,200–£1,800. An oil cooler seal replacement costs £800–£1,500. Check the Era Map above for your engine code.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-Benz ML250 BlueTEC / ML350 CDI reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: That depends on which engine and generation. The OM642 V6 (W164) has well-documented oil cooler seal failures and swirl flap issues. The OM651 four-cylinder (ML250 BlueTEC) had a timing chain tensioner recall and suffers from AdBlue/DPF/emissions system weaknesses. The W166 ML350 BlueTEC with the later OM642 is the most refined and reliable. Buy on history.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz ML250 BlueTEC / ML350 CDI?

acceptedAnswer.text: OM651 (ML250): timing chain tensioner oil leak (recall), AdBlue/DPF/emissions system failures. OM642 (ML350): oil cooler seal failure (oil/coolant contamination, £800–£1,500), swirl flap motor failure (loss of low-end torque, £500–£1,200), NOx sensor failure (£300–£800), EGR valve/cooler issues (£500–£1,200).

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-Benz ML250 / ML350 diesel engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: M-Class values range from £4,000–£20,000 depending on generation, condition and mileage. A £3,500–£6,500 reconditioned engine represents 20–80% of vehicle value — our 70% Mercedes-Benz scrap threshold means careful evaluation is required. For a well-maintained W166 ML350 BlueTEC, replacement is often justified. For an ageing W164 with other issues, assess scrap value first.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz ML250 / ML350 diesel engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The ML250 BlueTEC uses the OM651 engine code — a 2.1-litre four-cylinder diesel producing 201 bhp. The ML350 CDI (W164) uses the OM642 engine code — a 3.0-litre V6 diesel producing 224–231 bhp. The ML350 BlueTEC (W166) uses the OM642 engine code — a 3.0-litre V6 diesel producing 258 bhp. Check your VIN or registration year. The code is stamped on the engine block.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the OM642 oil cooler seal issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the oil cooler seals can be replaced (£800–£1,500). This is a well-known OM642 repair. Updated purple Viton seals are available. If you see oil in the coolant (mayonnaise in the expansion tank) or coolant in the oil, stop driving immediately and book a specialist inspection. Ignored seal failure can lead to bearing damage and engine failure.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz ML250 / ML350 Diesel Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect current market ranges across both M-Class diesel generations. OM651 used engines are more widely available; OM642 V6 units command a premium. A typical engine replacement in the UK can range from £3,067 to £6,105 or more. See the Era Map above for generation-specific pricing.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an ML250/ML350 diesel engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £75–£110/hr (independent Mercedes specialist) or £130–£180/hr (London/SE dealer).

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes-Benz ML250 / ML350 Diesel — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Recall repair — free if not already done
Essential repair — can cause engine failure if ignored

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Mercedes-Benz ML250 / ML350 Diesel? Check These First
buyIf: * W166 ML350 BlueTEC (2012–2015) with OM642 — the most refined and reliable M-Class diesel
* W164 ML350 CDI with documented oil cooler seal history
* ML250 BlueTEC with timing chain tensioner recall completed and emissions system history
* Full service history with regular oil changes and Mercedes-approved oil
* Post-2010 OM642 (updated purple Viton oil cooler seals)
avoidIf: * ML250 BlueTEC with no emissions system history — AdBlue/DPF issues are expensive
* ML250 BlueTEC with no timing chain tensioner recall documentation
* OM642 with evidence of oil/coolant contamination — oil cooler seal failure risk
* Poor service history — both engines are unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The ML250 BlueTEC and ML350 CDI are the core M-Class diesels — the OM651 four-cylinder and the OM642 V6. The ML350 CDI (W164) is a refined V6 but has oil cooler seal and swirl flap issues. The ML250 BlueTEC (OM651) is economical but has timing chain tensioner recall and AdBlue/DPF system weaknesses. The W166 ML350 BlueTEC with the later OM642 is the pick of the bunch — more power, better refinement, and improved reliability. Buy on history, maintain the emissions and oil systems, and the M-Class will serve you well.

cta.label: → Compare Mercedes-Benz ML250/ML350 diesel engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz ML250 BlueTEC / ML350 CDI (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for any rattle or rough running
* Service History — Check the year to identify which engine — see the Era Map above
* Oil / Coolant Check — Check expansion tank for mayonnaise (oil cooler seal failure — OM642)
* Timing Chain Tensioner Recall — ML250 BlueTEC: has the recall been completed?
* AdBlue / DPF System (ML250) — Any warnings? Check AdBlue level; emissions system history is critical
* Swirl Flap History (OM642) — Any loss of low-end torque? Swirl flap motor checked?
* NOx Sensors — Any engine warning light? NOx sensor codes are common
* EGR System — Any coolant loss or white smoke? EGR cooler failure is common
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Resolve before buying
